narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges like your lower-middle family income background, and we appreciate the courage it takes to reach this point. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 40% chance of failure for you—high enough that it led to the rejection of your application to protect both you and the school's outcomes. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking, which was in the bottom group (1st decile, well below the typical 5th or 6th decile), strongly pushing the model toward expecting bar exam struggles. That factor stood out as the single biggest influence, signaling to the model that your later academic performance raised concerns about handling the intense demands ahead. On a more positive note, your family income bracket actually worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success compared to students from higher-income homes. Your Year 1 decile (4th, a bit below average) and undergraduate GPA (2.60 versus the usual 3.21) also helped pull things toward passing, showing some foundational strengths. Even your LSAT score of 27, though lower than the average 37, contributed slightly positively in context. Being a part-time student had a minimal effect overall. Compared to typical applicants—who often have stronger deciles around the 5th to 6th, higher GPAs and LSATs, full-time status, and middle-to-upper incomes—your profile highlighted that Year 3 dip as unusually influential in tipping the scales. While this is disappointing, especially given barriers many Black women like yourself face in education, it's not the end of your path. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits or additional coursework, retake the LSAT for a higher score, and consider full-time options if possible. We encourage you to seek mentorship from successful Black lawyers or programs supporting underrepresented students—they can provide tailored guidance to help you pass the bar on your first try. Keep pushing forward; your resilience will shine through.
